text:
When the lottery balls fall and all six numbers match, many winners start spending. They may buy an Audi, book a holiday in the US, and call an estate agent. A study says this is common for lottery millionaires.

The UK National Lottery started in 1994. Since then, it has made 3,000 millionaires. Together they have won more than £8.5bn, which is about £2.8m each on average. The study by Oxford Economics says their money also helped create 3,780 more millionaires among their children, family, and friends.

Many winners stop working right away (59%). Some keep their jobs (19%). Others do unpaid volunteer work (31%). The study says 98% of winners’ spending stayed in the UK. It estimates that each winner’s spending supports six full-time jobs for one year.

The winners added almost £750m to GDP and created more than £500m in tax money for the Exchequer. Most money was spent on homes. Winners spent £2.72bn on their main homes, and £170m to pay off debts and mortgages.

Winners also wanted to keep an income. They spent £2.125bn on investments. They gave £1.17bn as gifts to family and friends. They spent £680m on cars and holidays.

Camelot, which runs the UK National Lottery, paid for the study to mark 3,000 winners. The study used research from 100 winners who won £1m or more. It found that all 3,000 winners bought 7,958 houses or flats in the UK, or 2.7 each on average, spending £3.3bn. Most winners (82%) moved to a new main home, spending about £900,000 on average.

Some winners added special things to their homes. Almost a third (29%) bought a hot tub. A walk-in wardrobe was chosen by 28%. Nearly a quarter (24%) chose a home behind electric gates. About 22% had a games room, and 7% put in a snooker table.

Big homes need care. About 30% hired a cleaner, and 24% hired a gardener. A small number (5%) hired a beautician.

Audi was the favourite car for 16% of winners. Range Rovers and BMWs were also popular (11% each). Mercedes were chosen by 10%, and Land Rovers by 5%. Winners spent £463m on 17,190 cars. The average price of their favourite car was £46,116.

Holidays were also important. Most winners (68%) chose five-star hotels overseas. The US was the top destination (27%), followed by the Caribbean (9%). Some winners also bought caravans in the UK. Over the past 18 years, 10% of millionaires bought a caravan, creating about £7.4m in sales.

Some winners (15%) started their own business. About 9% helped other people start one, and 6% invested in or bought other people’s businesses. Businesses started or supported by winners employ 3,195 people, the study says.

Andy Logan, who wrote the report, said the effect of a win spreads further than expected. He said it changes the lives of friends and family, and it also affects the UK economy because so much money is spent in the UK. He said each win can create a ripple effect for this generation and often the next.

The lottery balls have fallen, and all six numbers match. Now it is time to buy an Audi, book a holiday in the US, and call an estate agent. An analysis says many lottery millionaires do these things.

The UK National Lottery started in 1994. Since then, it has made 3,000 millionaires. Together, they have won more than £8.5bn in total. On average, each won £2.8m. The study authors, Oxford Economics, say this money also helped make 3,780 more millionaires among the winners’ children, family, and friends.

Most winners (59%) stop working right away. But 19% keep their job, and 31% do unpaid volunteer work. The study says 98% of winners’ spending stayed in the UK. By spending money on property, vehicles, and holidays, each winner is estimated to support six full-time jobs for one year.

The winners added almost £750m to gross domestic product (GDP). They also created more than £500m in tax for the Exchequer. Most money was spent on property. Winners spent £2.72bn on their main homes, and £170m to pay off debt and mortgages.

Many winners wanted to keep having income. They spent £2.125bn on investments. They gave £1.17bn as gifts to family and friends. They spent £680m on cars and holidays.

Camelot, the operator of the UK National Lottery, paid for the study to mark the 3,000-winners milestone. The study used research from 100 winners who won £1m or more. It found that the 3,000 winners bought 7,958 houses or flats in the UK, which is 2.7 each on average. They spent £3.3bn. Most winners (82%) changed their main home, spending an average of £900,000.

Many new homes had extra features. Almost a third (29%) wanted a hot tub. A walk-in wardrobe was important for 28%. Almost a quarter (24%) chose a home behind electric gates. Also, 22% had a games room, and 7% put in a snooker table.

Big homes need help. About 30% hired a cleaner and 24% hired a gardener. A small number (5%) hired a beautician.

Audi was the favourite car for 16% of winners. Range Rovers and BMWs were also popular (11% each), as well as Mercedes (10%) and Land Rovers (5%). Winners spent £463m on 17,190 cars. The average price of their favourite car was £46,116.

Holidays were also important. Most winners (68%) chose five-star hotels abroad. The US was the top place for 27%, followed by the Caribbean (9%). In the UK, caravan sales also benefited. Over the past 18 years, 10% of millionaires bought a caravan, with sales worth about £7.4m.

Some winners (15%) started their own business. Another 9% helped other people start businesses, and 6% invested in or bought other people’s businesses. Businesses started or supported by lottery winners employ 3,195 people, the study says.

Andy Logan, the report author, said the effect of a win spreads further than expected. It changes the lives of friends and family. It also has a clear effect on the UK economy, because so much money is spent in the UK. He said each win creates a ripple effect in this generation, and often the next one too.

text:
Angela Erdmann never met her grandfather. He died in 1946, six years before she was born.

On Tuesday 8th April, 2014, she told people about an unusual event. She received a message in a bottle, 101 years after her grandfather threw it into the Baltic Sea. People think it may be the world’s oldest message in a bottle. A museum in Germany gave it to her and is showing it to visitors.

Erdmann, who was 62, said it was a big surprise. A man came to her door and said he had mail from her grandfather. He said a message in a bottle had been found, and the name on the card was her grandfather’s name. The man was a genealogical researcher. He found Erdmann in Berlin after the letter was given to the International Maritime Museum in Hamburg.

The brown beer bottle had been in the water for 101 years. A fisherman named Konrad Fischer found it in his catch while fishing in the Baltic Sea near the northern city of Kiel. Holger von Neuhoff, a curator at the museum, said it was the oldest bottled message he had seen. He said the museum has older documents without bottles, but this is the oldest with both the bottle and the document. He said it was in very good condition.

Researchers think Erdmann’s grandfather, Richard Platz, threw the bottle into the sea in 1913, during a hike with a nature appreciation group. He was 20 years old then.

Most of the postcard could not be read. But the Berlin address on the front could be read. The note also asked the finder to send it to his home address.

Erdmann said her grandfather also put two stamps from that time in the bottle, so the finder would not have to pay. But he did not think it would take 101 years.

Erdmann said the message made her feel emotional. She did not know her grandfather because he died at age 54, six years before she was born.

She said she knew very little about him, but learned he was a writer. She said he was open-minded, believed in freedom, and believed people should respect each other. She said he did a lot for young people, and later traveled with his wife and two daughters. She said it was wonderful to learn where her family came from.

Erdmann said she also likes culture and traveling around the world, and she is open-minded too. She said what her grandfather taught his two daughters, her mother taught her, and she has taught it to her sons.

Even though she was happy to get the message, she said people should not throw bottles with messages into the sea today. She said the sea already has too many bottles and too much rubbish.

The message and bottle will be shown at Hamburg’s Maritime Museum until the beginning of May 2014. After that, experts will try to read the rest of the text. It is not clear what will happen to the bottle after that, but Erdmann hopes it will stay in the museum.

She said they want to add some photos with the bottle, so visitors can see the young man who threw it into the water.

On Tuesday 8th April, 2014, she talked about a special moment. She received news about a message in a bottle. Her grandfather had thrown the bottle into the Baltic Sea 101 years earlier. People think it is the world’s oldest message in a bottle. A museum in Germany gave it to Erdmann and is showing it there.

Erdmann, 62, said it was very surprising. A man came to her door and said he had mail from her grandfather. He said a message in a bottle had been found, and the name on the card was her grandfather’s. The visitor was a family-history researcher. He found her in Berlin after the letter was given to the International Maritime Museum in Hamburg.

The bottle was a brown beer bottle. It had been in the water for 101 years. A fisherman named Konrad Fischer found it in his catch in the Baltic Sea near Kiel. Holger von Neuhoff, a curator at the museum, said it was the oldest bottled message he had seen. He said the museum has older documents without bottles, but with the bottle and the document together, this was the oldest. He said it was in very good condition.

Researchers think Erdmann’s grandfather, Richard Platz, threw the bottle into the sea in 1913. He was hiking with a nature group. He was 20 years old then.

Much of the postcard could not be read. But the Berlin address on the front was clear. The card also asked the finder to send the note to his home address.

Erdmann said her grandfather also put two stamps in the bottle. This was so the finder would not have to pay. She said he did not think it would take 101 years.

Erdmann said the message made her feel very emotional. She did not know her grandfather because he died at age 54.

She said she learned he was a writer. She said he was open-minded and believed in freedom and in people respecting each other. She said he helped young people, and later travelled with his wife and two daughters. She said it was wonderful because she could see where her roots came from.

Erdmann said she also likes culture and travelling around the world. She said she is open-minded too. She said what her grandfather taught his two daughters, her mother taught her, and she then taught her sons.

Even though she was happy, she said she hopes people will not throw message bottles into the sea today. She said the sea is already full of bottles and rubbish.

The message and bottle will be on display at Hamburg’s Maritime Museum until the beginning of May 2014. After that, experts will try to read the rest of the text. It is not clear what will happen to the bottle later, but Erdmann hopes it will stay at the museum.

She said they want to show some photos with the bottle. This is so visitors can see the young man who threw the bottle into the water.

text:
A thick layer of bird droppings covers a shiny gold railing at one of Britain’s most expensive homes. Dead pigeons are inside. Mirrors are broken, and water runs through damaged walls and ceilings. This house is called The Towers. It is a £30 million mansion on “Billionaires’ Row” in north London. People have kept its damage secret until now.

The Towers is one of ten big houses on The Bishops Avenue. This street is in a very expensive part of London. These houses have been almost empty since they were bought about 25 years ago. It is believed they were bought for members of the Saudi Arabian royal family. The tall columns are breaking. The swimming pools, covered with mosaic tiles, are full of broken pieces of building. Nature has taken over, and owls now live there.

Many houses on this street look the same—empty and falling apart. Lloyds Bank says The Bishops Avenue is the second most expensive street in Britain. At the same time, more people in London are struggling to buy a home because prices rise 11.2% each year. But 16 mansions on the most expensive part of the street are sitting empty. Many are behind locked gates, with metal covers on the windows and messy, overgrown gardens. Guard dogs patrol some properties.

Across the street is another empty mansion worth £18 million, with smashed windows and walls covered in anti-climb paint. Another mansion has metal grilles on the windows and was sold for £20 million.

Even so, prices keep rising. A mansion called Dryades sold for £12 million in 2007 and is believed to be worth about £30 million today. Heath Lodge is worth £13 million today. It was sold in the late 1970s for £400,000. Heath Lodge is also known as the place where fashion tycoon Aristos Constantinou was murdered in 1984 by a silver bullet.

For people who struggle to pay for housing, these empty, ruined mansions can feel very upsetting. A security guard said it was frustrating to see such huge homes—big enough for dozens of people—slowly falling apart.

Rich royals from Nigeria and Saudi Arabia were some of the first to come to this road near Hampstead Heath. Iranians came after the fall of the shah. Now, Chinese buyers are coming after Russians and Kazakhs who have paid millions to own an address that estate agents say is as famous as the Champs Elysées and Rodeo Drive. Recently, two mansions were for sale for £65 million and £38 million. They promised Italian marble, lifts with leather padding, and luxury panic rooms.

But the empty homes are in bad condition. Outside, stone fountains are breaking and lawns have turned into muddy bogs. Inside one house, water drips through a large crystal chandelier onto a thick carpet that is rotting under parts of a fallen ceiling. Moss grows through broken bricks. Bathroom mirror tiles lie scattered. A swimming pool has about a foot of dirty water, and flowers are growing through the tiles. Wooden slats are pushing out from the sauna.

The most dramatic ruin is The Towers. It is a large mansion surrounded by many trees. Pigeons fill its high-ceiling rooms. Water pours through three floors, and algae has turned the walls bright green. The water drops into a huge, empty swimming pool in the basement. Unopened wooden crates marked “bullet-proof glass” show that the old owners were worried about security.

Today, very few people live full time on The Bishops Avenue. A guard outside one mansion said the owners were not there. A guard outside Royal Mansion would not say if anyone was home. At another house, a staff member warned about guard dogs. Magdy Adib Ishak-Hannah, an Egyptian-born private healthcare businessman with personal wealth of £45 million, said he is one of the few people who live there all the time.

“It’s not a friendly place where you can talk over the fence,” he said. “To be honest, I have never seen what my neighbours look like. Next door, a Saudi princess spent £35 million on a new house and I’ve never seen her. There are about three houses that are lived in 24/7 and half of the properties are occupied three to six months a year. The other half, who knows if they come or not?”

These broken, expensive homes show a culture where very rich people buy British property as investments. One Hyde Park, a building of apartments in Knightsbridge, is another example. More than half of its flats are registered with the council as empty or second homes.

Even so, people on the street are talking about building £5 million apartments instead of £50 million mansions, to try to bring people back.

Anil Varma, a local property developer, wants to rebuild one very valuable site on the avenue into 20 apartments. The building would have a concierge, maid service, a 25-metre pool, a spa, and a cinema.

“If you build a big house and try and sell for £30m to £40m, it won’t sell,” he said. “Locals won’t buy and so you have to bring in overseas buyers.”

But using these empty properties to help solve the housing crisis still seems far away. Andrew Harper, a local Conservative councillor, laughed when asked if some of the ruined homes could become affordable housing. He said the land price would be too high.

“Very wealthy people own property there,” he said. “Sometimes they live in them and sometimes they don’t.”

text:
One warm summer evening, I decided to swim near Doolin Pier in County Clare, Ireland. I moved there in 2012. A woman was in the water with Dusty, a dolphin. Dusty often swims with the same group of people.

Dusty came to Doolin around 2008. Hundreds of people have swum with her, so she can seem tame. She was even in an Irish tourist ad where a girl says she wants to touch a dolphin.

That evening, the woman was tickling Dusty’s belly. About 20 tourists and local people watched from the pier. Soon after I got into the water, Dusty left the woman and suddenly became very wild. Later I learned she is very protective of her space when she is with someone. Her tail slapped the water hard. At first I thought she was just showing off, but then I realized she might be angry.

I tried to swim back to the pier to get out. But very quickly, Dusty crashed into me with her nose. It was very strong and very painful, and she was moving fast. I was thrown forward.

People on the pier stared at me with their mouths open. Dusty stayed next to me, still slapping her tail. That scared me the most. I thought if she hit me with her tail, I could sink.

I reached the pier, but I could not climb out because I was hurt. I felt huge fear. I shouted for help. A man reached in and pulled me onto the steps. Then another man came and said he was an orthopaedic surgeon who worked with marine injuries. He had been driving into Doolin and saw what was happening. I was very cold and very worried. I feared bleeding inside my body. He checked me and told me he could not feel signs of that, but I probably had broken bones.

Later I learned my injuries were serious: six fractures in my spine, three broken ribs, and a damaged lung.

I stayed in hospital for five days. I wore a back brace for several weeks. I could not work for five months and had trouble moving, with stiffness and pain. text:
Some cities are trying unusual ideas to make city life better. These ideas include glow-in-the-dark trees, underground bike storage, and solar-powered bins. City living can be good, but it can also be hard to live in a green way. People in cities face pollution, traffic, and fewer green spaces. Inventors are trying new ways to help.

Pop-up parks
Some cities seem built more for cars than for people. A pop-up park is a simple idea. You use an empty parking space, a little money, and a few plants to make a small park. The PARK(ing) project started as an art experiment in San Francisco. Now it has spread around the world. People also make temporary city farms and ecology demonstrations. The movement has a day of action every September.

Subterranean storage
Not everyone in cities uses a car. Bicycles are becoming more popular. But people need a safe place to keep a bike. A Tokyo engineering company called Giken designed an “eco-cycle, anti-seismic underground bicycle park.” It is a round storage system that is only seven metres wide. It goes deep underground and can hold 204 bikes. Owners can get their bike by pressing a button. The system brings the bike back up in about 13 seconds.

Glow-in-the-dark trees

text:
More people are now against Western Australia’s shark cull. Thousands of people went to beaches across Australia to ask the state’s premier to stop it. RSPCA Australia and Richard Branson, the owner of Virgin Atlantic, also spoke against it.

The state began catching and killing sharks longer than three metres after what it called an “unprecedented” number of shark attacks on its coast. A 35-year-old surfer was killed in November 2013. He was the sixth person to die from a shark attack in two years.

But the Shark Attack File says that across all of Australia, there has been an average of one shark-related death each year for the last 50 years.

Kate Faehrmann from the Sea Shepherd Conservation Society spoke at a protest in Perth. She said the policy will not work. She said drumlines, which are used to catch sharks, can kill many animals. They can catch sharks of any size, and also dolphins, turtles, and other animals. She said this is why many people do not want the policy.

Thousands protested at Cottesloe Beach in Perth and Manly Beach in Sydney. Hundreds also protested at Glenelg in south-west Adelaide, and at beaches in Victoria and Queensland. Faehrmann said the protests showed that many Australians want sharks protected. She said that even with fear from movies like Jaws, many people are saying, “That’s their ocean. We respect them, we love them and we don’t want them killed.”

Anthony Joyce, a surfer who once had his foot caught in a shark’s mouth, said the number of sharks killed will not change much.

The state government has refused to give a running total of sharks killed. There have been reports that sharks smaller than three metres were released after being caught. Drumlines are floating drums fixed to the seabed, with bait on hooks under them.

Conservationists say there is no evidence the cull will reduce shark attacks on humans. They say no earlier cull used only drumlines. Researchers at the University of Western Australia say the recent increase in attacks may be linked to Western Australia having the fastest-growing population in Australia, not to more sharks.

Richard Peirce from the UK charity the Shark Trust said the cull will not work and could bring more predators close to the coast. He said leaving drumlines in overnight can attract other predators to the area because sharks and other animals may be hooked and injured.

Worldwide in 2012, there were 80 unprovoked shark attacks, and seven were fatal. Humans kill nearly 100m sharks each year. RSPCA Australia said it believes the cull is not justified. It said there is no evidence that more attacks are caused by more sharks. It said the increase fits with changes in population and human behaviour, with more people in the water.

Richard Branson told Fairfax Radio the policy is backfiring. He said the premier, Colin Barnett, may have thought it would help tourism, but it may do the opposite. He said it makes people think there is a big problem, and it may stop people from coming to Perth and the countryside around it. He said it will only worry people for no good reason.

Opposition to Western Australia’s shark cull has grown. Thousands of people went to beaches across Australia to ask the state’s premier to end the policy. RSPCA Australia and Virgin Atlantic owner Richard Branson also spoke against it.

The cull means catching and killing sharks longer than three metres. It started after what the state government called an “unprecedented” number of shark attacks on Western Australia’s coast. In November 2013, a 35-year-old surfer was killed. He was the sixth person to die from a shark attack in two years.

But the Shark Attack File says that across all of Australia, there has been an average of one shark-related death each year for the last 50 years.

Kate Faehrmann, a board member of the Sea Shepherd Conservation Society, spoke at a protest in Perth. She said the policy will not work. She said drumlines, which are used to catch sharks, can kill many animals. She said they can kill sharks of any size, and also dolphins, turtles, and other animals. She said this is why the community does not want the policy.

Thousands of people protested at Perth’s Cottesloe Beach and Sydney’s Manly Beach. Hundreds also protested at Glenelg in south-west Adelaide, and at beaches in Victoria and Queensland. Faehrmann said the protests showed many Australians want sharks protected. She said that even with fear from movies like Jaws, people are coming out in large numbers to say they respect sharks and do not want them killed.

Anthony Joyce, a surfer who once had his foot caught in a shark’s mouth, said the number of sharks they plan to kill will not make a difference.

The state government has refused to give an ongoing count of how many sharks have been killed. There have been reports that sharks smaller than three metres were released after being caught on drumlines. Drumlines are floating drums anchored to the seabed, with bait hanging on hooks under them.

Conservationists say there is no evidence the cull will reduce shark attacks on humans. They say no earlier cull used only drumlines. Researchers at the University of Western Australia say the recent increase in shark attacks in the state may be linked to Western Australia having the fastest-growing population in Australia, not to more sharks.

Richard Peirce, chairman of the UK conservation charity the Shark Trust, said the cull would not work and could even bring more predators closer to the coast. He said the government could have tried other ways to deal with shark and human interactions, but chose a plan that is ineffective and makes things worse. He also said drumlines can attract other predators, especially if the lines stay in the water overnight, because animals on the hooks may be injured.

Around the world in 2012, there were 80 unprovoked shark attacks, and seven were fatal. At the same time, humans kill nearly 100m sharks each year. RSPCA Australia said it believes the cull is not justified. It said there is no evidence that more attacks are caused by more sharks. Instead, it said the rise fits with changes in population and human behaviour, meaning more people are in the water.

Richard Branson told Fairfax Radio the policy was backfiring. He said Western Australia Premier Colin Barnett may have thought it would help tourism. Branson said it may do the opposite. He said it makes the problem look bigger than it is, and may stop people from coming to Perth and the countryside nearby. He said it could worry people for no good reason.
